title: Spillover: Animal Infections and the Next Human Pandemic
excerpt: A gripping investigation into zoonotic diseases—infections that jump from animals to humans—and why they pose the single greatest pandemic threat to humanity.
keyIdeas: Most emerging infectious diseases originate in wildlife and 'spill over' into human populations through complex ecological pathways
The convergence of habitat destruction, wildlife trade, agricultural intensification, and global travel creates unprecedented opportunities for pathogen emergence
Understanding disease ecology requires a One Health approach that connects human, animal, and environmental health
Pandemic preparedness is not just about vaccines and drugs but about surveillance at the human-animal interface
keyTakeaways: Zoonotic spillover is a predictable consequence of ecological disruption, not random bad luck
Bats are extraordinary viral reservoirs because their immune systems tolerate viruses without becoming sick
Early detection at spillover sites offers the best chance of containing emerging outbreaks before they globalize
The distinction between emerging and re-emerging diseases matters for surveillance strategy
Disease emergence is a numbers game: more human-animal contact means more opportunities for spillover
whoShouldRead: Public health professionals and epidemiologists seeking deeper understanding of disease emergence
Science journalists and communicators covering infectious disease outbreaks
Policy makers and global health leaders interested in pandemic prevention strategy
General readers who want to understand the origins of COVID-19 and what comes next
whoShouldSkip: Readers seeking graphic details about symptoms (Quammen is vivid but not gratuitous)
Those who prefer prescriptive policy solutions over narrative science journalism
Readers uncomfortable with the implication that pandemics are inevitable features of our ecological era
